mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Brazil on?
mapQuestions.answer: South America. Brazil is the biggest country on the continent and takes up almost half of it 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Brazil?
mapQuestions.answer: Brasília. Not Rio, not São Paulo. Brasília was built completely from scratch in just 4 years and became the capital in 1960, designed in the shape of an airplane when seen from above.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Brazil speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Portuguese. Brazil is the only country in South America that does not speak Spanish.

mapQuestions.question: How many countries does Brazil border?
mapQuestions.answer: 10. Brazil shares a border with every other South American country except Chile and Ecuador.

mapQuestions.question: What is the largest river in Brazil?
mapQuestions.answer: The Amazon. The Amazon carries about 20% of all the river water on Earth into the ocean. In some places it is so wide you cannot see one side from the other.

foods.name: Feijoada
foods.description: A black bean stew slow-cooked with smoked pork, sausage and beef. Brazil's national dish, traditionally eaten on Saturday lunch with rice, fried kale and orange slices.

foods.name: Pão de queijo
foods.description: Bouncy, chewy little cheese rolls made with cassava flour and Minas cheese. The smell when they come out of the oven is the smell of Brazil.

foods.name: Açaí bowl
foods.description: Frozen blended açaí berry from the Amazon, served like a thick purple sorbet with banana, granola, honey or condensed milk on top. Eaten for breakfast or after a beach session.

foods.name: Coxinha
foods.description: A teardrop-shaped fried pastry stuffed with shredded chicken (and sometimes cheese). Sold in every padaria (bakery) and snack bar in the country.

foods.name: Brigadeiro
foods.description: A small chocolate-fudge truffle rolled in chocolate sprinkles. Made by boiling condensed milk with cocoa and butter. Served at every birthday party in Brazil.

foods.name: Picanha churrasco
foods.description: A prime cut of beef rump cap (picanha), skewered, rubbed with rock salt, grilled over coals at a "churrascaria" steakhouse, and sliced onto your plate as it comes off the fire.

scavengerItems.label: A Brazilian football shirt (yellow with green details)
A samba drum, percussion group or carnival musician
The Christ the Redeemer statue (in person or as an image)
A real toucan, scarlet macaw, or any bright tropical parrot
A capybara (the world's biggest rodent — looks like a giant guinea pig)
A genuine Amazon-rainforest tree, a botanical garden palm, or a tropical mural
Someone playing volleyball or "futevôlei" (foot-volleyball) on a beach
A street artist or graffiti mural in São Paulo / Rio
A baroque colonial church with gold inside (Minas Gerais / Salvador)
A coconut water vendor on the beach with a machete
People playing capoeira in a circle on the street
Bossa nova or samba playing from a beach kiosk speaker

geoMatches.description: The "Marvellous City". Sugarloaf Mountain, Copacabana and Ipanema beaches, Christ the Redeemer, and the most famous Carnival on the planet.
The biggest city in South America, with 22 million in the metro area. Skyscrapers as far as the eye can see, the best food in Brazil, and a vibrant graffiti scene.
The world's largest rainforest, covering 60% of Brazil. Home to 10% of all known species, with a river so wide it sometimes looks like the sea.
The first capital of Brazil, on the north-east coast. The heart of Afro-Brazilian culture, with cobblestone colonial streets, drum schools, and capoeira on every corner.
A horseshoe-shaped wall of 275 waterfalls on the border with Argentina. Wider than Niagara, taller than Niagara, and dwarfs Victoria Falls in number.
The world's largest tropical wetland, in the centre-west of the country. Easier to spot wildlife than in the Amazon — capybaras, jaguars, caimans, macaws.

senses.smell: Charcoal smoke from a churrasco grill, ripe tropical fruit at a market, ocean breeze and sunscreen on the beach, fresh hot pão de queijo from a bakery, jungle wetness in the rainforest…
senses.hear: Samba drums on a Saturday afternoon, Portuguese which sounds like Spanish but isn't, "Tudo bem?", an entire bar screaming "GOOOOL!" when a goal is scored, parrots in the trees, beach volleyball thumps…
senses.taste: Cold thick açaí with banana, salty cheese bread, smoky picanha beef, strong tiny black coffees called "cafezinhos", ice-cold guaraná soda, melting brigadeiro…
senses.feel: Wet humidity in the rainforest, hot sand in Rio under bare feet, the bass of a passing samba drum in your chest, cool Atlantic spray on a cliff, sticky beach sweat…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How big is the Amazon, really?
stories.body: The Amazon rainforest covers 60% of Brazil and stretches into eight other countries too. It is the biggest rainforest in the world, four times bigger than the second-place one. The Amazon River carries about 20% of all the freshwater that reaches the world's oceans. In its widest parts it is over 40 kilometres across, so wide you cannot see one bank from the other. The forest is home to 10% of all the world's known species — jaguars, anacondas, piranhas, electric eels, pink river dolphins, sloths, harpy eagles, 1,300 species of birds, 40,000 plant species, and indigenous peoples who have lived there for thousands of years. The Amazon is sometimes called "the lungs of the planet" because the forest produces so much oxygen.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If the Amazon really is the lungs of the planet, who do you think should be in charge of protecting it?

stories.question: Why is Carnival such a huge deal?
stories.body: Every year, in the weeks before Easter, Brazil throws the biggest party on Earth. Carnival started as a Portuguese pre-Lent farewell-to-meat tradition, but African and indigenous rhythms turned it into something completely new. Rio de Janeiro's carnival is the most famous, with rival samba schools competing in the giant Sambódromo arena, where 70,000+ people watch dancers in towering feathered costumes. Around 2 million people fill the streets of Rio for free street parties called "blocos". Salvador in the north-east has even bigger street crowds. Every city, every town, every village has some kind of carnival. Schools close. Offices close. The whole country goes dancing for almost a week.

stories.question: Why is there a giant statue of Jesus over Rio?
stories.body: The statue of Christ the Redeemer (Cristo Redentor) on top of Corcovado mountain stands with arms outstretched over the city of Rio de Janeiro, 30 metres tall on top of an 8-metre base. It was built in the 1920s by a French sculptor and a Brazilian engineer, and finally unveiled in 1931. It is now one of the New Seven Wonders of the World. The statue gets struck by lightning several times every year — in 2014 one of its thumbs was knocked clean off during a storm. The clouds around the mountain often hide the statue completely, then suddenly part to reveal Jesus looking down at Copacabana beach.

stories.question: Why does Brazil speak Portuguese and not Spanish?
stories.body: In the 1490s, Spain and Portugal were both racing to claim land in the "new world" across the Atlantic. To avoid a war between two Catholic countries, the Pope drew a line straight down the middle of the Atlantic Ocean in 1494, the Treaty of Tordesillas. Everything west of the line went to Spain. Everything east of the line went to Portugal. Most of South America fell on the Spanish side of the line. But the eastern bulge of Brazil happened to fall on the Portuguese side. So Brazil ended up Portuguese, while every other South American country became Spanish. Today Brazil has over 200 million Portuguese speakers, far more than Portugal itself (10 million).
stories.thinkingQuestion: The Pope drew a single line and it changed the language of a whole continent. What other decisions like that have shaped your part of the world?

stories.question: How African is Brazil?
stories.body: From the 1500s to the 1880s, Brazil received more enslaved Africans than ANY other country — around 4 million people, about 40% of the entire Atlantic slave trade. (The USA, for comparison, received around 400,000.) Brazil was also the LAST country in the Americas to abolish slavery, in 1888. Today around 56% of Brazilians identify as Black or mixed-race. Brazilian culture — samba, capoeira (a martial art that looks like a dance, invented by enslaved people to disguise fight training as celebration), candomblé religion, much of Brazilian cooking, brigadeiro sweets — has huge African roots. The city of Salvador in the north-east is the most African-Brazilian city, with the largest community of African descendants in the Americas outside of Lagos, Nigeria.

convoQuestions.question: Best food you have eaten in Brazil so far?
Have you tried saying "obrigado" or "obrigada" to a stranger? What did they do?
Beach, jungle, city, mountain — which has felt the most "Brazil" so far?
Samba, bossa nova, funk, sertanejo — what music have you heard most often?
Brazilians are usually really warm to strangers. How has that felt compared to back home?
Brazil is bigger than the entire USA without Alaska. What does "huge" actually feel like here?

wordSearch.hint: Brazilian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Capuchin monkey
animals.description: In Tijuca Forest right above Rio, in any rainforest reserve, sometimes on city walls.

animals.name: Scarlet or blue-and-yellow macaw
animals.description: Loud, brilliantly-coloured parrots flying in pairs above the Amazon and Pantanal.

animals.name: Yacare caiman
animals.description: In Pantanal rivers and lakes — usually placid, often dozens in one stretch of bank.

animals.name: Capybara
animals.description: The world's biggest rodent — looks like a chilled-out giant guinea pig. Common in the Pantanal.

animals.name: Pink river dolphin
animals.description: In the Amazon. Yes, actually pink. Yes, real.

animals.name: Jaguar
animals.description: The Pantanal is the best place on Earth to spot a wild jaguar, lounging by the riverbank.
